coredata.dc:title: First principles study of the adsorption of hydrated heavy metals on graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, H.
Teleb, N.H.
Yahia, I.S.
Zahran, H.Y.
Elhaes, H.
Ibrahim, M.A.
coredata.dc:description: The adsorption of hydrated heavy metals on graphene quantum dots is investigated using the density functional theory. The considered heavy metals are Cd and Pb that are hexa-, penta-, and tetrahydrated with water molecules. Various adsorption schemes are considered such as surface and edge adsorption, and the interaction with functional groups attached to the flake's edges. The calculated positive adsorption energy implies that the considered graphene flakes are able to adsorb the hydrated heavy metals through all the proposed positions and interactions. Both physical and chemical adsorption have been observed, the physical adsorption is characterized by longer adsorption distance and lower adsorption energy with respect to the chemical adsorption. The adsorbed heavy metals have a significant effect on the electronic properties of the graphene flakes. The wide energy gap (∼3.7 eV) of the hexagonal flake decreases to ∼0.6 eV by surface-adsorption of hexahydrated Cd. This decrease occurs due to the inclusion of new energy states to the band structure which in turn leads to shifting of the highest occupied molecular orbital toward the Fermi level. The effect on the tiny band gap of the triangular flake is very small, the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals are unaffected by the adsorption process. The calculated total charge on the hydrated metals implies that the charge transfer strongly depends on the interaction scheme. The lowest charge transfer from tetrahydrated Pb to the triangular flakes is observed in case of surface adsorption, Q = −0.05 (e), and the largest charge transfer, Q = 1.1 (e), is observed in the adsorption of tetrahydrated Cd on 2NH-functionalized triangular flake.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DFT
Adsorption of hydrated heavy metals
Cd and Pb
Electronic properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Initial (In) and optimized (Opt) structures of hydrated Cd (a–c), and interaction schemes of AHEX with hydrated Cd and Pb(e-i).

The left hand side (LHS)shows the density of states and molecular orbitals for selected interactions between the hydrated Cd and the AHEX. The right hand side (RHS) shows similar interactions but with ZTRI.
(dXC) the distance between the adsorbed metal and the AHEX, the charge on the hydrated metal (Q), (Ea) the adsorption energy, (Eg) the energy gap, and TDM is the total dipole moment.
The same as in Table 1 but for ZTRI.

head.ce:title: First principles study of the adsorption of hydrated heavy metals on graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Spectroscopy Department, National Research Centre, El-Buhouth Str., Dokki, Giza, 12622, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The adsorption of hydrated heavy metals on graphene quantum dots is investigated using the density functional theory. The considered heavy metals are Cd and Pb that are hexa-, penta-, and tetrahydrated with water molecules. Various adsorption schemes are considered such as surface and edge adsorption, and the interaction with functional groups attached to the flake's edges. The calculated positive adsorption energy implies that the considered graphene flakes are able to adsorb the hydrated heavy metals through all the proposed positions and interactions. Both physical and chemical adsorption have been observed, the physical adsorption is characterized by longer adsorption distance and lower adsorption energy with respect to the chemical adsorption. The adsorbed heavy metals have a significant effect on the electronic properties of the graphene flakes. The wide energy gap (∼3.7 eV) of the hexagonal flake decreases to ∼0.6 eV by surface-adsorption of hexahydrated Cd. This decrease occurs due to the inclusion of new energy states to the band structure which in turn leads to shifting of the highest occupied molecular orbital toward the Fermi level. The effect on the tiny band gap of the triangular flake is very small, the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals are unaffected by the adsorption process. The calculated total charge on the hydrated metals implies that the charge transfer strongly depends on the interaction scheme. The lowest charge transfer from tetrahydrated Pb to the triangular flakes is observed in case of surface adsorption, Q = −0.05 (e), and the largest charge transfer, Q = 1.1 (e), is observed in the adsorption of tetrahydrated Cd on 2NH-functionalized triangular flake.

ce:section.ce:para: Elimination of heavy metals from wastewater is a worldwide concern due to the severe detrimental effects that they can cause to the human health [1–3] and to the surrounding environment [4]. For instance, exposure to Cd may results in lung cancer as well as various renal problems. Therefore, it is highly important to eliminate these toxic heavy metals from wastewater before its release into the environment. Graphene and its derivatives have been extensively employed for the removal of heavy metals from water and wastewater due to its high surface area, namely 2600 m2 g−1, tunable surface chemistry, and non-corrosive property [5–9]. One of the graphene derivatives is graphene oxide (GO) which is an oxidized form of graphene that is decorated with oxygen containing groups such as COOH, OH, and C = O on the surface. It has been reported that GO membranes are impenetrable to different liquids, vapors, and gases, but allow complete permeation of water [10]. These bizarre effect is attributed to the path of water molecules through the nanochannels formed between GO sheets, where it follows a tortuous path over the hydrophobic nonoxidized surface instead of the hydrophilic oxidized part of GO [11,12]. Moreover, the functionalization of GO can significantly enhance its adsorption capability of different pollutants from wastewater [13,14]. Another graphene derivative is the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which due to the quantum confinement [15–17] and edge morphology [18,19] can offer additional functionality to graphene for the adsorption of heavy metals. Moreover, the surface or the edges of GQDs can be chemically functionalized to increase the interactivity and tune the energy gap [20–22] which in turn enhances their adsorption capability. GQDs can be fabricated from graphene oxide by different types of reductions such as hydrothermal reduction [23], it can also be synthesis from graphene sheets with controllable sizes and well-defined edges [24]. Previous investigations on the adsorption of neutral and charged ions of heavy metals (Cd, Hg, and Pb) on the surface of hexagonal GQDs with zigzag termination showed that the adsorption of Pb is higher than that of either Cd or Hg [25]. Also a strong interaction between GQDs and heavy metals can be promoted by Vacancy engineering [26]. However, previous word did not discuss the effect of GQDs shape, edge termination, chemical functionalization, and hydration of heavy metals on the adsorption process. In this article, we investigate the capability of GQDs to adsorb heavy metals under the effect of the previously stated parameters.

Hydration of heavy metals have been attracting the attention of scientists from the end of the last century [27,28]. Raman spectroscopy confirmed the ab-initio quantum mechanical calculations of the hydration of Scandium (III) [29]. Raman spectroscopy also supported the computational results that describe the hydration of some ions including beryllium, chloride, nitrate, and sulfate [30]. Revealing mechanisms of the ion hydration vital for understanding the properties of electrolyte solutions [31]. The hydration of metals is also an important step in the metal ion interactions in the aquatic environment. It is stated that for the metals Cd, Cu, Pb and Zn, hydration is necessary before they interact with organic structures through hydrogen bonding of carboxyl group [32]. Different probable coordination number and structure for the first hydration shell of Pb(II) have been studied with density functional theory (DFT) as well as with polarized continuum models [33]. A mixed quantum mechanical charge field molecular dynamics have been carried out to study the hydration of Pb(II) [34]. The aim of this work was to gain a direct insight about the structural and dynamic aspects of the Pb(II) solvation. The results indicated a variation in the first shell coordination number from 7 to 9 in the Pb(H2O) n
2+ complex with Pb(H2O)8 2+ as a major species [34]. DFT combined with supermolecular/continuum solvent model has been used to study the hydration of Pb(II) [35]. It has been reported that, hexa- and heptahydration of Pb(II) exhibit the hemidirected symmetry. On the other hand, Pb(II) which is hydrated with eight and nine water molecules are corresponding to holodirected symmetry. For heavy metals in the aquatic environment, hydration is an important step toward the transport of metal. For example, in different methods of pollution control, the metal such as Pb needs to be hydrated before it undergoes adsorption, complexation or even a change in its oxidation state in order to be remediated from the environment [36–39]. Hydration is important not only for metal ions but also for other ligands and organic structures in the aquatic environment [40,41].

This article is conducted to study the ability of graphene quantum dots to remove hydrated heavy metals, Cd and Pb, from wastewater. The calculations are performed using DFT [42,43] as implemented in Gaussian 09 software [44]. The hybrid functional Becke-three parameters-Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) is employed in our calculations of the electronic properties [45,46] because it shows a good representation of the electronic structure of C-based nanosystems [47–49]. Two basis sets are used, the 3-21g for H, C, O, and N atoms which proved to be adequate when considering both the results accuracy and the computational power [50–52]. The second set is the aug-cc-pvdz-PP [53,54] for both Cd and Pb because they result in optimized structures that are considerably less hemidirected than it is predicted with the lanl2dz basis [55]. Heavy metals are considered in the wastewater environment as being hydrated by different number of water molecules. In particular, hexahydrated Cd represents a Cd atom surrounded by six water molecules and pentahydrated Pb represents a Pb atom covered by five water molecules. We consider different mechanisms for the adsorption of the hydrated heavy metals on graphene quantum dots: (a) adsorption on the surface and the edges of the GQDs (b) interaction with one and two unsaturated carbon atoms at the edges, (c) hydrated heavy metals interacting with functionalized GQDs. The adsorption energy and charge transfer are calculated to show the effect of GQD shape, edge, and chemical functionalization on the adsorption process.

Hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots have shown flexible electronic properties and enhanced interactivity by chemical functionalization [48]. Owing to these unique physical properties such class of compounds offers good possibilities for adsorption of hydrated heavy metals in the aquatic environment. In order to study their ability to adsorb heavy metals from wastewater, two flakes are selected, namely the hexagonal flake with armchair termination (AHEX) and triangular with zigzag termination (ZTRI) [56,57]. The AHEX is chosen to show the effect of its wide energy gap (Eg = 3.67eV) and armchair termination on the adsorption process while the ZTRI is chosen to study the effect of edge states (Eg = 0.31eV) and the zigzag termination on the adsorption. The following notation is used to describe the hydration: metal.nH2O, where considered metals are Cd and Pb and n = 4–6 represents the number of water molecules. For example, Cd.6H2O represents hexahydrated Cd and Pb.5H2O denotes pentahydrated Pb.

Various mechanisms of interaction between the hydrated metal and the chosen GQD are considered in Fig. 1 . Hexa-, penta-, and tetrahydrated Cd is shown before (In) and after optimization (Opt), in Fig. 1(a)-(c). The initial and optimized structures of Cd.6H2O adsorption on AHEX edge and surface are shown in Fig. 1 (d) and (e), respectively. The hydrated metal is situated at 4 Å from the AHEX edge or above its surface. Also, the initial and optimized structures resulting from the interaction of Cd.4H2O (Pb.5H2O) with two (one) unsaturated C atoms at the edges are shown in Fig. 1(f) (g). The interactions of functionalized AHEX flakes, namely AHEX_2COOH and AHEX_N, with Pb.5H2O and Pb.4H2O are presented in Fig. 1 (h) and (i), respectively. The hexagonal graphene flakes successfully adsorb the hydrated Cd and Pb in all the selected configurations. The calculated positive adsorption energy (Ea), in Table 1 , confirms the stability of the AHEX quantum dot and its ability to adsorb the selected heavy metals. Ea is given by the difference between the total energy of the hydrated metal + the total energy of the graphene flake and the total energy of the resultant structure. The adsorption energy can be significantly enhanced by chemically functionalizing the AHEX flake. For instance, when AHEX passivated with N atom (see Fig. 1 (i)), the adsorption energies of pentahydrated Cd and Pb increased to ∼ 8.2 and 9.5 eV, respectively. The effect of attaching 2 COO groups to the AHEX on enhancing the adsorption capability is greater than that by attaching 2NH groups where the N-H bonds in the later decrease the heavy metal adsorption by N atoms. The charge on the hydrated metals (Q = Qtotal- Qin, where Qtotal is the net charge of the hydrated metal after adsorption and Qin is the hydrated metal total charge before adsorption) is calculated based on the Hirshfeld charge analysis [58].

A positive value of Q, as given in Table 1, implies that electron charge is transferred from the hydrated metal to the GQDs. Our results of charge transfer are in good agreement with previous work on the adsorption of heavy metals by GO [14]. It is seen from Table 1 that the hydrated metals are electron donors and the AHEX flakes are electron acceptor except for the adsorption of Pb.5H2O by the AHEX functionalized by N atom. In this second case, The AHEX is the electron donor and the metal is the electron acceptor, with lowest charge transfer, because two of the hydrogen atoms from the hydrated metal leave it and form bonds to the N atom, as shown in Fig. 1 (i). Therefore, the partial positive charge on the H atoms, due to the absorption of part of its electron by the N atom, is now added to the AHEX total charge. Low charge transfer is observed in the cases of edge and surface adsorption and high amount of charge transfer is observed in chemically modified GQDs such as charge transfer from the tetrahydrated metal ((Q∼1 (e) for both Cd and Pb)) to the AHEX with unsaturated two C atoms. Additionally, charge transfer proportional to the adsorption energy and distance where high charge transfer corresponds to high adsorption energy and small adsorption distance as indicated in Table 1.

The two types of adsorption are observed: physical and chemical. The physical adsorption occurs for edge and surface adsorption of both Cd and Pb (Fig. 1 (d), (e)). The rest of the other interactions form chemical bond between heavy metals and the chemically modified GQDs. Large adsorption distance (dXC with X representing Cd or Pb and C representing carbon, as given in Table 1) characterizes the physical adsorption cases while small distances characterize the chemical adsorption ones. In addition to dxc, the effect of adsorption on the GQDs structure can be estimated by comparing the C - C, C - O, and C - N bond lengths before and after the adsorption process. The C - C bond length after adsorption of hydrated metals on the GQDs (from 1.39 to 1.47 Å) is almost the same like before adsorption (see Table 1 in Ref. [22]). The C - O bond length in the carboxylated GQDs increases after adsorption, for example it increases from 1.24 Å to 1.33 Å after adsorption of Cd.4H2O by ZTRI_2COOH which implies the C - O bond energies decreases after the interaction with the hydrated metals. Similarly, the C - N bond decreases in the nitrogen-functionalized GQDs after adsorption of heavy metals because the corresponding bond length increases, for example, from 1.29 to 1.46 Å after adsorption of Cd.4H2O by AHEX_2NH. In contrast to the increase of the bond lengths in carboxylated and nitrogenated GQDs, the C - O bond length in 2COO-functionalized flakes decreases after adsorption which means that the bond energy increase. For instance, one of the C - O bond lengths in ZTRI_2COO decreases from 1.42 to 1.32 Å after interaction with Cd.4H2O. The energy gap and the total dipole moment (TDM), presented in Table 1, will be discussed in the following subsection which investigates the effect of adsorption on the electronic properties of the graphene quantum dots.

Now we turn on to study the interaction of hydrated Cd and Pb with the ZTRI flake using similar interaction schemes proposed previously for AHEX. The graphical representation of these interactions is presented in Fig. 3 in Appendix A. Compared to the adsorption of hydrated Cd on AHEX; it is found that the adsorption energy of hexahydrated Cd by the ZTRI flake edge and surface is significantly higher, see Table 2 . This is a result of the smaller adsorption distances in ZTRI with respect to that in AHEX.

The adsorption energies of pentahydrated Cd on 2NH functionalized ZTRI and the unsaturated C atoms at the edges are comparable to their values in AHEX. The adsorption distances (dX-C) are also similar to those for the AHEX dot except for the edge adsorption case, where for adsorption of Cd.6H2O on ZTRI dXC = 2.78 Å which is smaller than dXC = 4.12 Å in AHEX. In case of ZTRI, we consider functionalization by COOH, instead of COO used in AHEX to see the effect of the additional H atom on the adsorption. Our results indicate that for the COOH functionalized ZTRI dot the adsorption energy (Ea = 4.91 eV, as in Table 2) is lower than the corresponding value (Ea = 7.96 eV) for COO functionalized ZTRI. Therefore, the additional H atom in COOH group decreases the adsorption of the metal by the O atoms. The positive values of charge, Q in Table 2, on the hydrated metals in all adsorption cases (except for adsorption of Pb.4H2O on ZTRI surface) signify that the hydrated metals are the electron donors while the ZTRI flakes are the electron acceptors. Similar to AHEX the largest charge transfer from the hydrated metals to the ZTRI flakes is observed for adsorption on functionalized flakes, namely Q∼1.1 (e) for the adsorption of pentahydrated Cd by ZTRI functionalized with 2NH groups. It is worth noting that the adsorption energy of Pb is higher than that of Cd [10,59,60] in all the considered interactions in both the ZTRI and AHEX except for the adsorption of tetrahydrated metals where the adsorption of Cd is a bit higher than Pb. This could be due to the higher deformation in the tetrahydrated Pb (Fig. 3 l) with respect to that in tetrahydrated Cd (Fig. 3 i), the high adsorption distance of two water molecules in the first case leads to a lower adsorption energy of the overall tetrahydrated Pb.

In this section, we study the influence of heavy metals interactions with GQDs on the electronic properties, namely the induced changes in the electronic density of states (DOS), the energy gap, and the total dipole moment (TDM). The DOS is calculated using a Gaussian distribution
1
2 π
α
2
exp
[
−
( ε −
ε
i
)
2
2
α
2
]
, representing each energy level
ε
i
with broadening
α ∼ 0.03 e V .
The Fermi level is fixed at zero energy by shifting the whole electronic energy spectrum by EF = (EHOMO + ELUMO)/2, with EHOMO representing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy and ELUMO being the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy. Tables 1 and 2 show the energy gap and TDM of the hexagonal and triangular graphene flakes before and after the adsorption. Apparently, the energy gap (Eg) in AHEX experiences a considerable decrease after the adsorption. For example, Eg decreased from 3.67 eV in AHEX to 0.63 eV in AHEX-Cd.6H2O and 1.83 eV in AHEX-Cd.5H2O compounds. In the first case of surface adsorption of hexahydrated Cd, the energy gap decreases due to the inclusion of new energy states to the energy spectrum due to the interaction between AHEX and the hydrated Cd, theses states lead to shifting of HOMO toward the Fermi level. These energy states can be noticed by comparing the DOS plots for AHEX before and after the adsorption shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) , respectively, where the HOMO shifted from E ∼ -2 eV in Fig. 2(a) to E ∼ -0.5 eV after adsorption as in Fig. 2 (b). The HOMO-8 shown in Fig. 2 (d) represents one of the additional energy states from the hydrated Cd that leads to the decrease of the band gap. The negligible distribution of HOMO over the hydrated metals due to the physical adsorption (no electron sharing) confirms the small adsorption energy obtained for this case. In the case of adsorbing pentahydrated Cd on an unsaturated C atom at the edge of AHEX, the considerable decrease of Eg occurs as a result of the system transformation from antiferromagnetic state in AHEX to ferromagnetic state in the AHEX-Cd.5H2O system. When the system is in the ferromagnetic state the energy gap is given by the difference between the beta LUMO and the alpha HOMO which is significantly smaller than that for the antiferromagnetic AHEX. The interaction between the 2COO functionalized AHEX and the tetrahydrated Cd forms strong chemical bonds between O and Cd atoms, which results in the DOS in Fig. 2 (h) having a wide energy gap and the molecular orbital describing that bond (HOMO-9 as in Fig. 2 (j)) having low energy in the valance band.

The influence of heavy metals adsorption on the density of states and the molecular orbitals of the ZTRI is shown in the right hand side (RHS) of Fig. 2. What makes the DOS of ZTRI different from the AHEX ones is the appearance of edge states. Four edge states shown by red color in Fig. 2 (k), result in a very small energy gap in the ZTRI. These four edge states are the HOMO, HOMO-1, LUMO, and LUMO+1. The HOMO, LUMO, and the energy gap between them are unaffected by the adsorption, however the HOMO-1 shifted to lower energies as seen in Fig. 2 (l). Similar to AHEX, the physical adsorption of Cds.6H2O on the surface of ZTRI result in a negligible distribution of HOMO and LUMO on the metal as shown in Fig. 2 (m), (n). The nonzero total spin obtained in ZTRI.Cd5H2O contains contributions from the same number of edge states as in ZTRI, although these states split into alpha states and beta states as shown in Fig. 2 (o). The HOMO and LUMO are edge states for both the alpha and beta orbitals (as seen from the pi anti-bonding molecular orbitals in Fig. 2 (p)). The strong chemical bond formed between Cd and ZTRI edge carbon atom is represented by the HOMO-5 energy state in Fig. 2 (q). The small energy gap characterizing the ZTRI dot greatly increases in this system because now it is the difference between the alpha LUMO and beta HOMO energies. When ZTRI_2COOH adsorbs Cd.4H2O, the energy separation between the four edge states increases and now the four peaks can be clearly seen in Fig. 2 (r). The molecular orbital representing the chemical bonding between Cd atom and O atom from the functionalized ZTRI is HOMO-16 (Fig. 2 (t)).

The electronic density of states and selected molecular orbitals of GQDs after the adsorption of hydrated Pb are presented in Fig. 4 in Appendix B. In general, the effect of Pb adsorption on GQDs leads to changes in the density of states similar to those occur by Cd. The HOMO (HOMO-2) is the molecular orbital describing one of the bonds (physical and chemical) between AHEX (ZTRI) and the hydrated Pb.

The TDM increases from zero (D) in AHEX to higher values after adsorption. For example, the TDM∼7 (D) for AHEX_2NH-Cd.4H2O compound. Giant values of TDM are observed only in the cases of adsorption by ZTRI. It can be seen in Table 2 that the TDM value increases from ∼0.89 (D) before adsorption to TDM = 24 (D) after adsorption in ZTRI_2COOH-Cd.6H2O. The considerable increase in the charge transfer and the enhanced TDM increase the interactivity of the GQDs to absorb additional metals.

The adsorption capability of hydrated Cd and Pb on graphene quantum dots has been investigated using DFT calculations. Various adsorption schemes have been studied to show the ability of graphene flakes to adsorb the hydrated heavy metals through different positions and interactions. Namely the following schemes are considered: (a) edge and surface adsorption, (b) interaction with unsaturated carbon atoms at the edges, and (c) adsorption on the edges of functionalized flakes. The calculated positive adsorption energy shows that graphene flakes have the capability to adsorb hydrated Cd and Pb, through all the considered interactions. The highest adsorption energy has been found in chemically modified flakes where metals are chemically adsorbed, for example the adsorption of Cd.4H2O by O atoms in the AHEX_2COO and the adsorption of Cd.4H2O on the edge of ZTRI. Charge transfer is observed between the hydrated metals and the graphene flakes; this transfer greatly increases after functionalization. The adsorption distance (distance between Cd or Pb atom and nearest carbon atom) are almost the same in the cases of chemical adsorption, from 2.2 to 2.5 Å, and considerably increases in cases of physical adsorption, from 2.5 to 4.1 Å. The adsorption process has a strong effect on the electronic properties of the resultant systems. The surface adsorption of Pb.6H2O decreases the energy gap from Eg∼3.7 to Eg∼0.6 eV in AHEX. This considerable decrease of the band gap occurs due to the inclusion of new energy states to the energy spectrum from the hydrated Cd that shift the HOMO toward the Fermi Level. In the case of ZTRI which is characterized by tiny energy gap due to the edges states, the adsorbed Cd or Pb does not interact with edge electrons and therefore the effect of interaction on the ZTRI energy gap is negligible. The total dipole moment is strongly enhanced as a result of the adsorption process. This increases the interactivity of the graphene flakes with hydrated heavy metals and can lead to avalanche enhancement of adsorption of additional heavy metals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their appreciation to “The Research Center for Advanced Materials Science (RCAMS)" at King Khalid University for funding this work under grant number RCAMS/KKU/003-18. The authors are very thankful to V. A. Saroka for the careful reading of the manuscript and the proposed corrections.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The initial (in) and optimized structures of hexa-, penta-, tetrahydrated Pb are shown in Fig. 3 (a)-(c). The optimized structures resulting from the interactions of hydrated Cd with ZTRI are shown in Fig. 3 (d)-(h) and that resulting from the interactions of hydrated Pb with the ZTRI are shown in Fig. 3(i)-(o)
Fig. 3
(a-c) Initial (in) and optimized (opt) structures of hydrated Pb, (d-o) optimized structures of hydrated Cd and Pb adsorbed by ZTRI flake through various physical and chemical adsorptions.
Fig. 3

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The density of states and selected molecular orbitals of hexagonal and triangular GQDs after interactions with hydrated Pb.
Fig. 4
The same as in Fig. 3 but for hydrated Pb.
Fig. 4

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular spectroscopic study of river nile sediment in the greater Cairo region”
